findings.title: Run quarterly premortems to pre-identify failure paths before they occur
findings.claim: Imagining your agency has already failed and working backward to identify the causes is more valuable than planning for success, because it reveals the few paths that guarantee failure — which are easier to avoid than success paths are to replicate.
findings.what_they_do: Four times a year, Dylan sets a company target, personally lists every pitfall that would guarantee failure, has every team leader independently do the same, then the group meets to reconcile lists and build mechanisms to avoid each failure mode. He also applies this when designing client offers: lists everything that would guarantee a client never gets their desired result, then builds the service to remove each obstacle.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented quarterly planning cadence, premortem or otherwise (§10 THIN, §9 GAP). Strategic review is ad-hoc and Josh/Evan-led with no formalized team input structure. This is a direct gap in Palm's ops maturity.
findings.recommendation: Introduce a quarterly premortem session: Josh + Evan (+ any team leads) each independently list failure modes for one target goal (e.g., 'what would guarantee we stay stuck at current revenue'), then meet to merge and prioritize. Build at least one concrete mechanism per top-3 failure mode before the quarter begins. Also apply premortem when scoping new Palm services (in-house chatting, AI creator line) to surface blind spots early.

sources.quote: there are many ways to fail but there are fewer ways to guarantee failure the beautiful thing about finding the few ways that guarantee failure is that you can easily avoid them

findings.title: Map specific failure modes to each revenue plateau ($10k→$100k) before hitting them
findings.claim: Agencies stall at revenue plateaus because they never pre-identified the specific failure modes for that stage — doing so in advance lets you build avoidance mechanisms before the ceiling hits.
findings.what_they_do: Dylan uses the premortem framework explicitly calibrated to the current revenue stage: if at $10k targeting $100k, he lists every reason it would stay at $10k (lead gen failure, low conversion, bad chatting, poor retention, inability to deliver results) and builds systems to prevent each before they occur.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented financial modeling or plateau-stage analysis (§7 GAP, §founder-discipline THIN). There is no written breakdown of what failure modes apply at Palm's current stage vs. the next tier.
findings.recommendation: Define Palm's current revenue stage and next target. Run one premortem session scoped specifically to 'what would guarantee we never reach [next target]' — specifically interrogating lead gen (§6 GAP), conversion, chatting quality (§8 OUTSOURCED risk), and creator retention (§9 GAP). Document outputs as a living risk register.

sources.quote: let's just say you got an only fans management agency that's doing 10K a month right and your goal is to get to 100K a month right we're going to now imagine that we didn't get to 100K a month and we got stuck at 10K a month forever and it failed

findings.title: Expand from marketing-only to full-service (adding chatting + ops) to outcompete partial-solution agencies
findings.claim: Agencies that solve more of the client's problems in a single offer — marketing, chatting, infrastructure, sales, fulfillment — generate dramatically more revenue than those offering only one or two services.
findings.what_they_do: Dylan explicitly transitioned from a 'basic marketing agency for OnlyFans creators' to a full-service management agency that includes chatting, marketing, software/infrastructure, sales support, and fulfillment. He credits this expansion (informed by a premortem of what clients needed) with scaling to millions in revenue.
findings.vs_us: Palm currently covers social marketing (§1 MATURE) and outsources chatting (§8 OUTSOURCED). OF vault/PPV production is THIN (§2). Competitor is already offering the full stack Palm is building toward. The gap is chatting (in-house roadmap ~1yr out) and a more integrated PPV/vault production system.
findings.recommendation: Use the premortem technique to formally list everything Palm's creators need that Palm does NOT currently provide in-house. Prioritize in-house chatting (already on roadmap) and OF vault content production (§2 GAP) as the highest-leverage expansions. Frame the full-service offer explicitly in creator acquisition pitches (§6) as a competitive differentiator vs. agencies that only do social.

sources.quote: I went from my basic marketing agency for only fans creators to a full service management agency offering things like chatting as well as marketing and a ton of other stuff

findings.title: Have every team leader independently write failure factors before group discussion to avoid groupthink
findings.claim: Independent individual brainstorming before group discussion surfaces more diverse and honest failure modes than a top-down or group-first process.
findings.what_they_do: In Dylan's quarterly premortem, every leader in the organization writes their own failure list independently first. Only after individual lists are complete does the team convene to share, discuss, and prioritize — preventing any single voice (including the CEO's) from anchoring the group prematurely.
findings.vs_us: Palm's team structure is thin (§10 THIN) and strategic planning is not formalized. There is no documented process for soliciting structured input from editors, Amin, or the external chat team on operational risks.
findings.recommendation: When running Palm's first premortem, require Evan, Josh, and any team leads (editors, eventually chat manager) to submit written failure lists independently via a shared Airtable form or doc before any sync call. Use this to surface risks the CEO may be blind to, particularly around editor throughput, posting reliability (Amin dependency), and chat team quality (§8 OUTSOURCED risk).

sources.quote: every leader in my organization writes the obstacles that would guarantee failure as well then we meet and figure out how to avoid these failures

findings.title: Design service tiers by listing what would guarantee the client never gets results, then removing each obstacle
findings.claim: The strongest agency service offer is built by exhaustively listing the obstacles that would guarantee a creator fails, then structuring the offer to remove each one — producing a 'no-brainer' that outclasses competitors offering only partial solutions.
findings.what_they_do: Before designing a client offer, Dylan lists every factor that would guarantee the client never achieves their desired transformation (e.g., no TikTok traction, no chatting, no sales system). He then designs a mechanism — the service package — that explicitly removes each obstacle. The offer's value proposition is framed as obstacle removal, not feature delivery.
findings.vs_us: Palm's creator onboarding captures basic data (§6 THIN) but there is no documented process for scoping what a specific creator needs or for designing a customized service package. Pricing/commission structure is also undocumented (§7 GAP). There is no premortem-style offer design process.
findings.recommendation: Before signing a new creator, run a lightweight premortem on their specific situation: list the 5–10 most likely reasons they would fail on OF under Palm's management, then ensure Palm's onboarding explicitly addresses each. Use this as both a service-design tool and a sales tool — showing the prospective creator that Palm has already mapped and solved her likely failure modes is a strong differentiator in acquisition conversations (§6).

sources.quote: I highly recommend doing a premortem when creating your service as it will lead you to create an amazing service while most of your competitors are stuck only offering partial solution you'll be 10 steps ahead solving problems your clients aren't even aware of yet

findings.title: Apply Theory of Constraints to identify and fix single bottleneck before anything else
findings.claim: An OFM agency will only grow until it hits its weakest constraint, so operators should identify that one limiting factor, eliminate it, then move to the next.
findings.what_they_do: Dylan's agency runs every scaling decision through Theory of Constraints: at any given time, identify the single biggest bottleneck (e.g., lead gen → signing → chatting quality), focus 100% of effort on eliminating it, then repeat. They explicitly deprioritize non-constraint improvements (e.g., website redesign, branding) until the real constraint is solved. The diagnostic question is: 'Why don't we have 10× more customers or make 10× more money?' — the answer is the constraint.
findings.vs_us: No internal baseline for a systematic prioritization framework at Palm (§11 / §0). Palm is currently running multiple parallel initiatives (SMM hub redesign, AI warmup, invoicing, whale-hunting, chatting roadmap) without a documented single-constraint lens to sequence them. Risk of spreading effort across non-constraint work.
findings.recommendation: Adopt a standing agenda item at Palm leadership (Josh + Evan) each sprint/month: 'What is our single biggest constraint right now?' Force-rank current initiatives against the three growth levers (more creators, higher creator value, lower risk). Explicitly park lower-impact work until the top constraint is resolved. Document the current constraint in the ops wiki so all team members share context.

sources.quote: a system will only grow until it's constrained to its limit
if you want to grow a business you have to sell more clients make them worth more or decrease risk

findings.title: Use 'likelihood × effect size' filter to rank which problems to solve
findings.claim: When evaluating competing problems, prioritize by multiplying likelihood of impact by effect size — not by ease or visibility.
findings.what_they_do: Dylan explicitly applies a two-axis filter: (1) how likely is solving this to move the needle, and (2) how large is the effect if it does? He uses website color changes as a counter-example — low likelihood of affecting model sign rates, therefore not a constraint regardless of how easy it is to fix. High-likelihood + large-effect-size problems get all the attention.
findings.vs_us: No documented prioritization rubric at Palm (§10 / §11 gap). Decisions appear to be founder-judgment-driven (Josh/Evan) without a shared scoring lens, which creates risk of effort going to high-visibility but low-impact tasks.
findings.recommendation: Create a lightweight decision filter for Palm's sprint planning: score each proposed initiative on Likelihood (1–5) × Effect Size (1–5). Anything below a threshold score gets parked. This is especially useful now as Palm juggles SMM hub, AI warmup, invoicing, and chatting roadmap simultaneously.

sources.quote: if there's another problem that has a much higher likely impact in terms of it's higher likely and it's actually got a greater effect size then I'm going to prioritize that

findings.title: Treat model lead gen as the first constraint to solve before any other agency system
findings.claim: If you cannot generate leads and have quality conversations with models, no other agency system (content, chatting, branding) matters — lead gen is the foundational constraint.
findings.what_they_do: Dylan frames the agency growth sequence explicitly: model acquisition (lead gen + signing) must be solved first. Without a flow of signed models, investing in chatting quality, content systems, or branding is wasted effort. He identifies 'can't find models, can't have quality conversations, can't close deals' as distinct sub-problems within acquisition that each need diagnosing.
findings.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is currently Josh-DM-led with no systematic machine (§6 — flagged as biggest GAP). There is no documented lead gen funnel, outbound cadence, or qualification criteria. This aligns with Dylan's framing: if Palm's constraint is model acquisition, all other system-building is secondary until this is solved.
findings.recommendation: Palm should formally assess whether model acquisition is currently its binding constraint. If yes (which §6 suggests), pause or deprioritize lower-leverage ops work and redirect Josh's attention to building a repeatable acquisition system: outbound DM cadences, referral incentives, or paid acquisition. Document the current signed-creator pipeline velocity as a KPI baseline.

sources.quote: if a constraint for you is I can't sign models like I can't like there's no traffic in terms of like my lead gen sucks I'm not going any leads I can't sign any models
if you don't have leads coming in then you're not going to be able to sign models to deliver results to make money

findings.title: Identify chatting quality as the natural next constraint after model acquisition is solved
findings.claim: Once model lead gen is fixed, chatting quality becomes the next bottleneck — unhappy clients signal the chat constraint needs addressing before further scaling.
findings.what_they_do: Dylan sequences agency growth constraints explicitly: first fix lead gen/signing, then when that's flowing, the next natural constraint becomes chatting performance (creators/clients unhappy with chat results). He treats each constraint as a stage gate — you don't work on chatting until acquisition is solved, but you also don't ignore chatting quality signals once acquisition is stable.
findings.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting to an external team today (§8) with no internal SOP, scripts, or performance benchmarks. Palm has no documented way to detect when chatting quality has become a constraint (e.g., no chatter KPIs, no client satisfaction signal). This is a build-toward gap for the planned in-house chat team.
findings.recommendation: As Palm builds toward an in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap), define now what 'chatting quality constraint' looks like as a measurable signal: creator churn rate attributable to low DM revenue, PPV conversion rate benchmarks, or client satisfaction scores. Build these KPIs into the whale-hunting / Fan Tracker system (§9) so Palm can detect the chatting constraint before it becomes a crisis.

sources.quote: maybe your chatting isn't that good and maybe your clients are not that happy with the chatting so that's going to be the next natural constraint

findings.title: Explicitly deprioritize non-constraint improvements to avoid wasted ops effort
findings.claim: Spending time on non-constraint improvements (website, branding, emails) while the primary constraint is unsolved is a common agency failure mode that stalls growth.
findings.what_they_do: Dylan's agency maintains explicit discipline around *not* working on improvements that don't address the current constraint. He gives concrete examples: not sending more emails, not redesigning the website, not fixing branding — all of which are improvements but not constraints. The team's attention is budgeted only toward the identified constraint until it's eliminated.
findings.vs_us: Palm is running several parallel workstreams simultaneously (SMM hub redesign, AI persona warmup, invoicing automation, whale-hunting, chatting roadmap — §11). Without a constraint lens, there is risk that high-effort but non-constraint projects are consuming Evan/Josh bandwidth that should go elsewhere. The SMM hub redesign, for instance, may be a 'website color' equivalent if acquisition is the real constraint.
findings.recommendation: Before Palm's next sprint, run a constraint audit: list all active workstreams, score each against 'does this directly address our #1 growth bottleneck today?' Any workstream that scores low should be paused or handed to a contractor. Protect founder bandwidth for constraint-critical work only.

sources.quote: there's lots of things I could do but what are the few things or one thing that matters most which links back to what is the constraint of the business
rather than like doing a bunch of pointless stuff that doesn't matter

findings.topic: Closing rate as a distinct constraint from lead gen
findings.title: Separate 'can't generate leads' from 'can't close leads' as distinct diagnosable constraints
findings.claim: Low signing rates have two distinct root causes — insufficient leads vs. inability to close available leads — and each requires a different fix.
findings.what_they_do: Dylan breaks the acquisition constraint into sub-constraints: (A) not enough leads at all, or (B) leads exist but close rate on calls is low. He treats these as separate problems requiring separate solutions. If you're jumping on calls daily but not closing, that's a closing/sales constraint, not a lead gen constraint — and conflating them leads to the wrong fix.
findings.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led (§6) with no documented funnel metrics. Palm likely has no visibility into whether the bottleneck is lead volume or close rate, since neither is measured. This makes it impossible to diagnose which sub-constraint to fix.
findings.recommendation: Palm should instrument the acquisition funnel with two basic metrics: (1) leads contacted per week, and (2) conversion rate from conversation to signed contract. Track these in Airtable (`Creators` table or a new `Acquisition Pipeline` table). Once measured for 4 weeks, the data will reveal whether the constraint is lead gen or closing — and Palm can then target the right solution (outreach volume vs. sales training/script).

sources.quote: maybe you can't close deals maybe that's the constraint maybe you've got a ton of leads but for some reason you just can't close them like you jumping on calls every day and you just can't close them

findings.title: Sequence first hires: running mate → exec assistant → chatter/chat agency
findings.claim: Founders should hire in a deliberate sequence—running mate first, then admin/exec assistant to offload repetitive tasks, then solve the chatting side—rather than trying to do everything at once.
findings.what_they_do: DylanOFM recommends: (1) find a running mate/right-hand who splits roles (one owns marketing, one owns chatting); (2) once income starts, hire an exec assistant/admin/rover to replace founder on all repetitive day-to-day tasks; (3) only after marketing is 'cracked' (~6–12 months) bring chatting in-house. The logic is sequential focus: solve one constraint at a time.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented org chart, hiring criteria, or sequencing plan (§10 GAP). Josh is currently acquisition-led and Amin handles manual posting; beyond that the team structure is thin. Palm has no written answer to 'who do we hire next and in what order.'
findings.recommendation: Document a Palm-specific hiring sequence. Given current state (outsourced chatting, Josh-led acquisition, Evan on tech), the logical next hires are: (1) an acquisition/sales person to scale creator signing (§6 GAP), (2) an ops/admin rover to free Josh from repetitive tasks, then (3) an in-house chat manager once the chatting roadmap matures (§8). Write this down as a living org plan.

sources.quote: I'd hire a running mate first and we would learn the entire marketing thing so we wouldn't have to go hire an organic marketer I would crack that I'd go and work with a chat agency straight away

findings.title: Use a 'running mate' trial period before granting business-partner equity
findings.claim: Before giving someone a 50/50 partnership, have them serve as a running mate first so you can assess loyalty, work ethic, and alignment before granting equity.
findings.what_they_do: DylanOFM had Jack work as his running mate (wearing multiple hats, proving himself) before Jack became CEO and equity partner. The running mate role is a structured proving ground: the person handles whatever the founder needs, often splitting the agency into a marketing half and a chatting half between the two of them. Equity and executive title come only after demonstrated performance.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented co-founder/partner vetting process or equity-granting criteria (§10 GAP). If Palm ever brings on a second operational leader, there is no framework for how to trial them first.
findings.recommendation: Adopt a formal 'running mate' trial structure for any senior operational hire: define a 90-day proving period with explicit KPIs before any equity conversation. Document the role split (e.g., one person owns social/acquisition, one owns operations/chat) so expectations are clear from day one.

sources.quote: if it's your business your idea you're the one that's putting in the resources to start and you want to get a business partner maybe have them as your running mate first before you actually make them like a 50/50 business partner

findings.title: Hire an admin rover to replace founder on all repetitive non-leverage tasks
findings.claim: A founder should not do any task that isn't directly moving the needle toward millions in revenue or massive influence; an admin/rover handles all else.
findings.what_they_do: The admin/exec assistant/rover role is trained across multiple functions of the org so they can plug wherever needed. They handle all day-to-day busy work—scheduling, coordination, follow-ups—freeing the founder to focus only on high-leverage activity (acquisition, strategy, key relationships). This person often becomes a foundational team member over time.
findings.vs_us: Palm does not have a documented admin/ops-coordinator role. Evan covers tech/ops and Josh covers strategy/acquisition, but there is no generalist rover absorbing repetitive tasks from both (§10 GAP). As Palm's creator roster and AI persona pipeline grow, this gap will create a bottleneck.
findings.recommendation: Define and hire a part-time ops coordinator/rover (could start as a VA) whose explicit job is to absorb repetitive tasks from both Josh and Evan: creator comms, invoice follow-ups, scheduling coordination with Amin, Airtable data hygiene. Write a task-capture log for one week to identify what to delegate first.

sources.quote: the reason that this can be a good first hire is because it's going to replace yourself of all repetitive tasks

findings.title: Hire a dedicated organic content marketer to own social trends, strategy, and collabs
findings.claim: An organic content marketer is a distinct hire responsible for staying current on platform trends, developing creator content strategies, and organizing influencer/collab partnerships.
findings.what_they_do: This person: monitors platform algorithm changes and best practices continuously; builds content strategies per creator; tracks audience behavior; maintains brand voice consistency; coordinates cross-creator collabs and influencer partnerships. They are not an editor—they are a strategist who feeds the content pipeline with direction.
findings.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) is data-driven and mature on the ideation/scraping side, but the system is tech-automated rather than human-curated. There is no person whose full-time job is monitoring trends, adapting strategy, and coordinating collabs. The 8-step pipeline is strong but has no human strategist continuously optimizing it. Real-creator posting cadence is also not codified (§1 GAP).
findings.recommendation: Consider adding a content strategist role (could be part-time initially) who owns the human judgment layer on top of Palm's automated pipeline: reviewing viral scores, calling cadence adjustments, and managing any collab or S4S opportunities. This person would also close the real-creator posting cadence gap (§1).

sources.quote: an organic content marketer is going to stay up to date with the latest social media trends they're going to step up to date with platform updates and the best practice

findings.topic: Chat agency as a learning vehicle before in-house build
findings.title: Deliberately use a chat agency partnership to reverse-engineer their systems before building in-house
findings.claim: Working with a chat agency at 15% cost is worth it not just for focus but as a structured way to learn their SOPs, hiring, and sales scripts before you build your own team.
findings.what_they_do: DylanOFM paid a chat agency 15% (and a sales agency 25%) explicitly to observe and document their internal operations—how they hire, train, script, and sell—then used that knowledge to build his own in-house version. He frames giving away margin as tuition for hands-on operational learning, more valuable than buying a course.
findings.vs_us: Palm currently outsources chatting to an external team (§8, deliberate current state). The in-house chat team is ~1 year out on the roadmap. Palm should be actively documenting what the current external chatting partner does—their scripts, fan-tier logic, PPV sequencing—as build-toward intelligence. If this documentation isn't happening, Palm is paying for a service but not capturing the learning value.
findings.recommendation: Formalize a 'reverse-engineering protocol' with the current external chat team: have someone at Palm (Evan or a designated ops person) request access to or documentation of their SOPs, scripts, fan-tiering approach, and PPV sequencing. Even partial visibility now will dramatically accelerate the in-house chat build in 12 months. Treat the current outsourcing arrangement as paid MBA tuition, not just a vendor relationship.

sources.quote: go and work with a chat agency even though you're not going to make as much money it can be really good in terms of focus and clarity and then the next year you can go take what you've learned from working with the chat agency and go and build your own chatting team in house

findings.title: Chat manager role covers: content scheduling, chatter hiring/training, QA, custom chasing, and promo cadence
findings.claim: A chat manager's scope goes far beyond managing conversations—they own the full OF-page operation including content scheduling, chatter QA, customs follow-up, and ensuring a promotion is always running.
findings.what_they_do: Per DylanOFM, a chat manager is responsible for: scheduling content to the OF feed, hiring and training chatters, QA-ing chatter performance (are they showing up and working?), chasing custom content requests, and ensuring at least one promotion is live at all times on the page. This is effectively an OF page general manager role.
findings.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) and Palm has no internal SOP or role definition for a chat manager. When Palm builds the in-house chat team (~1 year out), this role definition is a concrete starting point. The 'promotion always running' requirement is especially notable—Palm has no documented promo cadence (§7 GAP).
findings.recommendation: Use this role definition as a draft job description for the future in-house chat manager hire. Specifically add: (1) 'promotion always running' as a standing KPI, (2) custom-content chase SOP, (3) chatter QA checklist. Start drafting these SOPs now even before the hire, so they're ready when the in-house build begins.

sources.quote: having a chatting manager is going to be actually a crucial part of the agency because they will take care of everything when it comes to the only fans pages as a whole and tasks such as scheduling content for the feed hiring chatters training chatters making sure that chatters are actually tuning up and working

findings.title: Hire a sales person early to close creator deals or free the founder to close
findings.claim: Without someone focused on closing creators (models), the agency can't generate revenue, making sales personnel one of the most critical early hires.
findings.what_they_do: DylanOFM treats sales and marketing as the two most important early functions, inseparable. A sales hire either closes creator deals directly or handles enough admin/prep that the founder can focus time on closing. The frame is: no closed creator deals = no revenue = no business. This person is specifically focused on the agency's supply side (signing creators), not fan-facing sales.
findings.vs_us: Palm's creator acquisition is currently Josh-DM-led with no systematic machine (§6 GAP). There is no documented sales hire, closer role, or outbound acquisition process beyond Josh's personal outreach and referrals. This is Palm's most significant scaling constraint on the supply side.
findings.recommendation: Prioritize a creator acquisition/sales hire as Palm's next headcount addition. Define the role: outbound DM outreach to potential creators, qualification criteria, pitch script, and handoff to Josh for final close. Even a part-time VA running outbound prospecting would multiply creator pipeline. Pair with a documented pitch/close SOP (§6 GAP) so the hire isn't starting from zero.

sources.quote: sales is one of the core skills of any business if you can't sell you can't make money and obviously in the ofm business if you can't close models if you can't close deals with models then you're not going to make any money
